source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A coin spinning about its axis of symmetry with angular frequency $\omega$ is set down on a horizontal surface (Fig. 1.148). After it stops slipping, with what velocity does it roll away?

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A coin spinning about its axis of symmetry with angular frequency $\omega$ is set down on a horizontal surface (Fig. 1.148). After it stops slipping, with what velocity does it roll away?
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image fields contain PIL object representations rather than explicit file paths or URLs
metadata.reasoning_chain: Take coordinates as shown in Fig. 1.149. Before the coin stops slipping, the frictional force is $f=\mu mg$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of sliding friction. Let $x_{c}$ be the $x$ coordinate of the center of mass of the coin. The equations of motion of the coin before it stops slipping are $$m\ddot{x}_{c}=-\mu mg$$ and $$I\ddot{\theta}=-\mu mgR,$$ where $m$ and $R$ are respectively the mass and radius of the coin, and $I=\frac{1}{2}mR^{2}$. Integrating and using initial conditions $\dot{x}_{c}=0$, $\dot{\theta}=\omega$ at $t=0$, we have $$\dot{x}_{c}=-\mu gt$$ and $$\dot{\theta}=\omega-\frac{2\mu gt}{R}.$$ When the coin rolls without slipping, we have $$\dot{x}_{c}=-\dot{\theta}R.$$ Suppose this happens at time $t$, then the above give $$-\mu gt=-\omega R+2\mu gt$$ or $$t=\frac{\omega R}{3\mu g}.$$ At this time, the velocity of the center of mass of the coin is $$\dot{x}_{c}=-\mu gt=-\frac{1}{3}\omega R,$$ which is the velocity with which the coin rolls away without slipping.
